name: Cusco Megaliths
description: The Cusco Megaliths refer to a collection of massive, precisely-cut stone structures found in and around Cusco, Peru, particularly at sites like Sacsayhuamán, Q'enqo, and Ollantaytambo. Proponents of alternative theories suggest that these structures were built by a pre-Inca civilization with advanced technology, possibly predating known history by thousands of years. They point to the immense size and weight of the stones, the precision of their joints, and the supposed lack of readily available technology in Inca society as evidence. Some even speculate about lost technologies like stone softening or anti-gravity devices. Mainstream archaeology attributes the construction of these sites primarily to the Inca civilization, with some evidence suggesting pre-Inca cultures contributed to earlier phases. They argue that while the engineering feats are impressive, they are achievable with known Inca techniques, including the use of ramps, levers, and a large workforce. The precision of the stonework is attributed to careful planning, skilled labor, and iterative refinement. The debate centers on the extent of Inca ingenuity versus the possibility of lost or unknown technologies, and the degree to which pre-Inca cultures contributed to the megalithic construction.
subtitle: Ancient stoneworks & alternative histories

claims.claim: The megaliths were built by a pre-Inca civilization with advanced technology.
claims.evidence: Immense size and weight of the stones; precision-cut joints; lack of mortar; polygonal masonry.
claims.criticism: Mainstream archaeology attributes construction to the Inca, using ramps, levers, and skilled labor. Polygonal masonry can be achieved through careful planning and iterative refinement.

claims.claim: Inca technology was insufficient to move and precisely place such massive stones.
claims.evidence: Lack of evidence for advanced tools or machinery; difficulty replicating construction techniques with known Inca technology.
claims.criticism: Experimental archaeology has demonstrated the feasibility of moving and placing large stones using known Inca methods, albeit with a large workforce and significant time investment.

claims.claim: The megaliths exhibit evidence of lost technologies, such as stone softening or anti-gravity.
claims.evidence: Unusual shapes and interlocking features; apparent inconsistencies with known stone-cutting techniques.
claims.criticism: These features can be explained by natural weathering, geological formations, and the Inca's mastery of stone shaping and fitting. There is no scientific evidence for stone softening or anti-gravity technologies in ancient Peru.

keyFigures.name: Brien Foerster
keyFigures.role: Promoter of the pre-Inca advanced technology theory.

keyFigures.name: Jean-Pierre Protzen
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who studied Inca stoneworking techniques.

keyFigures.name: Vincent Lee
keyFigures.role: Architect and researcher of Inca construction methods.

mythologyConnections: Viracocha (creator god associated with advanced knowledge)
Inca creation myths involving stone beings

timeline.event: Inca expansion and construction of major megalithic sites like Sacsayhuamán and Ollantaytambo.
Spanish conquest and early observations of the megalithic structures.
Rise of alternative theories attributing the megaliths to pre-Inca civilizations with advanced technology.
